question: [File: 'File Name: src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom / take] Can an unprivileged market creator deploying a fee-on-transfer loan token under a freshly created market trigger take() followed by withdraw(), where safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, address(this), buyerAssets - sellerAssets) receives strictly less than buyerAssets - sellerAssets due to the transfer fee, while marketState.withdrawable is incremented by the full accounting amount, violating the invariant that marketState.withdrawable <= actual loanToken balance held by Midnight, causing scoped impact: every subsequent withdraw() call for any lender in that market reverts with an ERC20 transfer failure once the real balance is exhausted? Proof idea: deploy a MockFeeOnTransferToken with a 1% fee, create a market, execute take() to build withdrawable, then call withdraw() for the full withdrawable amount and assert the safeTransfer reverts.

[File: 'File Name: src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom / repay] Can an unprivileged borrower calling repay() with a fee-on-transfer loan token under preconditions where the token deducts a fee on every transferFrom trigger a state where position[id][onBehalf].debt is reduced by units and marketState.withdrawable is increased by units, but Midnight only receives units * (1 - fee_rate) tokens, violating the invariant that sum(withdrawable) <= loanToken.balanceOf(Midnight), causing scoped impact: all lenders' withdraw() calls eventually revert with insufficient balance once the gap accumulates across multiple repayments? Proof idea: fuzz fee_rate in [1, 500] bps, call repay() N times, assert loanToken.balanceOf(midnight) < marketState.withdrawable after N repayments.

[File: 'File Name: src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom / supplyCollateral + withdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged borrower supplying collateral via supplyCollateral() with a fee-on-transfer collateral token under preconditions where position[id][onBehalf].collateral[collateralIndex] is incremented by the full assets amount but Midnight only receives assets * (1 - fee_rate) tokens trigger a permanent DoS on withdrawCollateral() for that borrower, because safeTransfer(collateralToken, receiver, assets) will attempt to send more than Midnight holds, violating the invariant that position.collateral[i] <= actual collateralToken balance attributable to that market, causing scoped impact: borrower's collateral is permanently frozen in the accounting but unrecoverable? Proof idea: deploy FeeCollateral with 0.5% fee, supplyCollateral(1000), assert withdrawCollateral(1000) reverts.

[File: 'File Name: src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer / withdraw] Can an unprivileged attacker who controls the receiver address in withdraw() pass a receiver that is blacklisted by the loan token (e.g., USDC/USDT-style blocklist) under preconditions where the lender has valid credit and marketState.withdrawable >= units trigger a permanent DoS on that lender's withdraw() because safeTransfer(loanToken, receiver, units) reverts, violating the invariant that a lender with valid credit and sufficient withdrawable must always be able to exit, causing scoped impact: lender's credit is permanently frozen if they cannot change the receiver? Proof idea: deploy a MockBlocklistToken, blacklist the receiver, call withdraw() and assert revert; then verify no alternative exit path exists.

[File: 'File Name: src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer / liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator calling liquidate() with a collateral token that reverts on transfer to specific addresses under preconditions where the borrower is unhealthy and the liquidator specifies a receiver that the collateral token rejects trigger a DoS on liquidate() because safeTransfer(collateralToken, receiver, seizedAssets) reverts before the loan token transferFrom is reached, violating the invariant that unhealthy positions must remain liquidatable, causing scoped impact: unhealthy borrower cannot be liquidated, bad debt accumulates, and lender credit is at risk? Proof idea: deploy a MockRevertingCollateral that reverts on transfer to address(0xdead), set receiver to that address, call liquidate() and assert NotLiquidatable is never the revert reason but the token revert propagates.

[File: 'File Name: src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer / claimSettlementFee] Can an unprivileged actor who has previously accumulated claimableSettlementFee for a token that later becomes non-transferable (e.g., paused, blacklisted receiver, or self-destructed) trigger a permanent DoS on claimSettlementFee() because safeTransfer(token, receiver, amount) reverts, violating the invariant that accrued protocol fees must always be claimable, causing scoped impact: settlement fees are permanently locked in the contract with no recovery path since claimSettlementFee has no alternative transfer mechanism? Proof idea: deploy a PausableToken, accumulate fees via take(), pause the token, call claimSettlementFee() and assert revert.

[File: 'File Name: src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer / claimContinuousFee] Can an unprivileged actor exploiting a loan token that reverts on transfer when the recipient is a specific address under preconditions where continuousFeeCredit > 0 and the feeClaimer sets receiver to a rejected address trigger a DoS on claimContinuousFee() because safeTransfer(loanToken, receiver, amount) reverts after marketState.continuousFeeCredit, totalUnits, and withdrawable have already been decremented, violating the invariant that fee accounting state must not desync from actual token transfers, causing scoped impact: continuous fee credit is permanently destroyed from accounting without any token being transferred? Proof idea: deploy a MockRejectReceiver token, call claimContinuousFee() with a rejected receiver, assert the state decrements occurred but the transfer reverted.

[File: 'File Name: src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer / withdraw + rebasing token] Can an unprivileged market creator deploying a rebasing loan token (e.g., aToken or stETH-like) under preconditions where the token's balanceOf(Midnight) decreases due to a negative rebase event trigger a DoS on withdraw() for all lenders because safeTransfer(loanToken, receiver, units) will revert when the contract's actual balance is less than the sum of all withdrawable units, violating the invariant that marketState.withdrawable <= loanToken.balanceOf(Midnight), causing scoped impact: all lenders in the market are permanently unable to withdraw their credit? Proof idea: deploy a RebasingToken, create a market, lend, trigger a 10% negative rebase, then call withdraw() for the full withdrawable amount and assert revert.

[File: 'File Name: src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom / take + zero-amount revert] Can an unprivileged taker calling take() with a loan token that reverts on zero-amount transferFrom under preconditions where the settlement fee equals the offer price (buyerPrice == settlementFee, so sellerAssets == 0) or where units is dust-level such that sellerAssets rounds to 0 trigger a DoS on take() because safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, receiver, 0) reverts, violating the invariant that all valid offer fills must be executable, causing scoped impact: offers near the settlement fee boundary become permanently unfillable, blocking market activity? Proof idea: deploy a ZeroRevertToken, set settlement fee to match offer tick price, call take() with 1 unit and assert revert.

[File: 'File Name: src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom / take + zero-amount revert on fee transfer] Can an unprivileged taker calling take() with a loan token that reverts on zero-amount transferFrom under preconditions where the settlement fee is 0 (settlementFeeCbp0 through settlementFeeCbp6 all zero) trigger a DoS on take() because safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, address(this), buyerAssets - sellerAssets) where buyerAssets == sellerAssets results in a zero-amount transfer that reverts, violating the invariant that take() must succeed for any valid offer with zero settlement fee, causing scoped impact: all take() calls in zero-fee markets are permanently blocked? Proof idea: deploy ZeroRevertToken, create market with all settlementFeeCbp = 0, call take() and assert revert on the zero-amount transferFrom.

[File: 'File Name: src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer / liquidate + collateral token self-destructs] Can an unprivileged attacker who creates a market with a collateral token that self-destructs after market creation trigger a permanent DoS on liquidate() because safeTransfer(collateralToken, receiver, seizedAssets) calls SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer which checks token.code.length > 0 and reverts with NoCode(), violating the invariant that unhealthy positions must remain liquidatable, causing scoped impact: all borrowers in the market become permanently unliquidatable, bad debt cannot be realized, and lender credit is permanently at risk? Proof idea: deploy a SelfDestructableToken, create a market, open borrower positions, self-destruct the token, call liquidate() and assert NoCode() revert.

[File: 'File Name: src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom / take + loan token self-destructs] Can an unprivileged attacker who creates a market with a loan token that self-destructs after market creation trigger a permanent DoS on take(), repay(), and liquidate() because safeTransferFrom checks token.code.length > 0 and reverts with NoCode(), violating the invariant that all core market actions must remain executable for the market's lifetime, causing scoped impact: the entire market is permanently frozen with no recovery path for any participant? Proof idea: deploy SelfDestructableLoanToken, create market, self-destruct token, call take() and assert NoCode() revert.

[File: 'File Name: src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer / withdraw + transfer returns false] Can an unprivileged market creator deploying a loan token whose transfer() returns false (without reverting) under preconditions where a lender attempts to withdraw trigger a DoS on withdraw() because SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer checks returndata.length == 0 || abi.decode(returndata, (bool)) and reverts with TransferReturnedFalse(), violating the invariant that a lender with valid credit and sufficient withdrawable must always be able to exit, causing scoped impact: all withdraw() calls permanently fail for that market? Proof idea: deploy a FalseReturnToken, create market, lend, call withdraw() and assert TransferReturnedFalse() revert.

[File: 'File Name: src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom / repay + transferFrom returns false] Can an unprivileged market creator deploying a loan token whose transferFrom() intermittently returns false under preconditions where a borrower attempts to repay trigger a DoS on repay() because SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom checks the return value and reverts with TransferFromReturnedFalse(), while position[id][onBehalf].debt has already been decremented and marketState.withdrawable has already been incremented before the transfer, violating the invariant that debt reduction must be atomic with token receipt, causing scoped impact: repay() is blocked and the accounting desync (debt reduced, no tokens received) permanently corrupts the market's solvency? Proof idea: deploy a ConditionalFalseReturnToken, call repay() when the token is set to return false, assert TransferFromReturnedFalse() and verify debt was already decremented.

[File: 'File Name: src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom / take + ERC777 tokensReceived hook reentrancy] Can an unprivileged taker using a loan token with ERC777-style tokensReceived hooks under preconditions where the taker's callback contract implements tokensReceived and reenters take() during safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, receiver, sellerAssets) trigger a state where the outer take() has already updated consumed[maker][group] and position state but the inner reentrant take() observes stale state, violating the invariant that consumed amounts must be monotonically increasing and offer caps must not be exceeded, causing scoped impact: offer overfill or double-credit creation via reentrancy through the ERC20 transfer hook? Proof idea: deploy ERC777LoanToken, implement tokensReceived to reenter take() with same offer, assert consumed[maker][group] exceeds offer.maxUnits after both calls complete.

[File: 'File Name: src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer / liquidate + ERC777 tokensReceived hook reentrancy on collateral] Can an unprivileged liquidator using a collateral token with ERC777-style tokensReceived hooks under preconditions where the liquidator's receiver contract implements tokensReceived and reenters liquidate() for the same borrower during safeTransfer(collateralToken, receiver, seizedAssets) trigger a double-liquidation where the second call observes the already-reduced collateral and debt state, violating the invariant that a single liquidation sequence must not allow the same borrower's position to be liquidated twice in one transaction, causing scoped impact: borrower's remaining collateral is seized beyond what the protocol allows, or bad debt is double-realized? Proof idea: deploy ERC777CollateralToken, implement tokensReceived to reenter liquidate() for same borrower, assert total seizedAssets across both calls exceeds original collateral.

[File: 'File Name: src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom / take + payer equals receiver self-transfer revert] Can an unprivileged taker calling take() where the computed payer equals the receiver (e.g., buyerCallback == address(0) and offer.buy == false so payer = msg.sender and receiver = offer.receiverIfMakerIsSeller == msg.sender) with a loan token that reverts on self-transfer (from == to) trigger a DoS on take() because safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, receiver, sellerAssets) where payer == receiver reverts, violating the invariant that all valid offer fills must be executable, causing scoped impact: specific offer configurations become permanently unfillable? Proof idea: deploy SelfTransferRevertToken, construct offer where payer == receiver, call take() and assert revert.

[File: 'File Name: src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer / withdraw + Midnight address blacklisted] Can an unprivileged attacker who controls the loan token (e.g., as market creator using a custom ERC20) blacklist the Midnight contract address in the token's blocklist under preconditions where lenders have valid credit trigger a permanent DoS on withdraw() because safeTransfer(loanToken, receiver, units) calls token.transfer(receiver, units) from Midnight's address, and some tokens revert when the sender is blacklisted, violating the invariant that lenders must always be able to exit, causing scoped impact: all withdraw() calls for that market permanently revert, freezing all lender credit? Proof idea: deploy a BlacklistToken, blacklist address(midnight), call withdraw() and assert revert.

[File: 'File Name: src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom / take + Midnight address blacklisted as spender] Can an unprivileged attacker who controls the loan token blacklist the Midnight contract address as a spender under preconditions where takers attempt to fill offers trigger a permanent DoS on take() because safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, address(this), buyerAssets - sellerAssets) calls token.transferFrom(payer, midnight, amount) where Midnight is the spender, and tokens that revert when the spender is blacklisted will block all take() calls, violating the invariant that valid offers must be fillable, causing scoped impact: the entire market's take() function is permanently blocked? Proof idea: deploy a SpenderBlacklistToken, blacklist address(midnight) as spender, call take() and assert revert.

[File: 'File Name: src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom / liquidate + loan token blacklists liquidator] Can an unprivileged borrower who controls the loan token (or colludes with the token issuer) blacklist a specific liquidator's address under preconditions where the borrower is unhealthy trigger a DoS on liquidate() because safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, address(this), repaidUnits) reverts when payer (the liquidator) is blacklisted, violating the invariant that unhealthy positions must remain liquidatable by any eligible liquidator, causing scoped impact: targeted liquidators are blocked, unhealthy positions persist, and bad debt accumulates? Proof idea: deploy a BlacklistLoanToken, blacklist liquidator address, call liquidate() and assert revert after collateral has already been transferred out.

[File: 'File Name: src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer / liquidate + collateral transfer before loan token pull] Can an unprivileged borrower exploiting the ordering in liquidate() where safeTransfer(collateralToken, receiver, seizedAssets) executes before safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, address(this), repaidUnits) under preconditions where the collateral token transfer succeeds but the loan token transferFrom reverts trigger a state where collateral has been sent to the receiver but no loan tokens were received, violating the invariant that collateral seizure must be atomic with loan token repayment, causing scoped impact: liquidator receives collateral for free if the loan token transfer can be made to revert after the collateral transfer? Proof idea: deploy a ConditionalRevertLoanToken that reverts on the second call, call liquidate() and assert collateral was transferred but loan tokens were not received.

[File: 'File Name: src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom / take + max transfer limit token] Can an unprivileged market creator deploying a loan token with a per-transaction maximum transfer limit under preconditions where a taker attempts to fill a large offer trigger a DoS on take() because safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, address(this), buyerAssets - sellerAssets) reverts when the amount exceeds the token's max transfer limit, violating the invariant that all valid offer fills must be executable regardless of amount, causing scoped impact: large offers become permanently unfillable, blocking efficient market operation? Proof idea: deploy a MaxTransferToken with limit = 1000e18, create offer with maxAssets = 10000e18, call take() with units that require > 1000e18 transfer and assert revert.

[File: 'File Name: src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer / withdraw + token reverts on transfer to contract address] Can an unprivileged taker or lender calling withdraw() with a receiver that is a contract address under preconditions where the loan token reverts on transfer to contract addresses (some tokens implement this as an anti-bot measure) trigger a DoS on withdraw() because safeTransfer(loanToken, receiver, units) reverts, violating the invariant that lenders must be able to withdraw to any valid receiver address, causing scoped impact: lenders using smart contract wallets or aggregators as receivers are permanently blocked from withdrawing? Proof idea: deploy a NoContractReceiverToken, call withdraw() with receiver = address(someContract) and assert revert.

[File: 'File Name: src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom / repay + callback ordering + fee-on-transfer desync] Can an unprivileged borrower calling repay() with a callback under preconditions where the loan token is fee-on-transfer trigger a state where position[id][onBehalf].debt is decremented by units and marketState.withdrawable is incremented by units before the callback executes, but the callback's onRepay() receives units as the repay amount while the actual token transfer only delivers units * (1 - fee_rate) to Midnight, violating the invariant that the repay callback's reported units must match the actual tokens received, causing scoped impact: the callback contract may make decisions based on the inflated units value, leading to accounting errors in integrating protocols? Proof idea: deploy FeeOnTransferLoanToken and a RepayCallbackChecker, assert onRepay receives units but midnight.balanceOf increases by only units*(1-fee).

[File: 'File Name: src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer / claimContinuousFee + state decremented before transfer] Can an unprivileged actor exploiting the ordering in claimContinuousFee() where marketState.continuousFeeCredit, totalUnits, and withdrawable are all decremented before safeTransfer(loanToken, receiver, amount) executes under preconditions where the loan token transfer reverts trigger a permanent accounting desync where the fee credit is destroyed from state but no tokens are transferred, violating the invariant that continuousFeeCredit must equal the actual claimable token amount, causing scoped impact: protocol fee revenue is permanently lost from accounting with no recovery path? Proof idea: deploy a ConditionalRevertToken, call claimContinuousFee() when token is set to revert, assert continuousFeeCredit was decremented but no tokens were transferred.
